I tried to save and load model as I usually do when using sklearn model, but the evaluation result of model is apparently not the same. Anything wrong with my code?

save model
`tree = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=1, min_samples_leaf=1)
  bag = Bagging(base_classifier=tree, n_classifiers=10)
  bag.fit(text, labels)
 with open(bag_path, "wb") as f:
            pickle.dump(bag, f)
`

load model
`with open(self.bag_path, "rb") as f:
            self.bag = pickle.load(f)`

This is a planned content:
1. Update README to include the capacity report feature
2. Refine the round_it utilities.

issue_title: PyPi package is not up to date
issue_body:
Hi! I just compared your github codebase to the your PyPi package (installed via pip) and noticed that the code there seems to be out of date (even though it gives the same version number).

Would be cool to have an up-to-date version on pypi since there's a problem with ascii escape sequences in the pypi package that doesn't seem to exist with the github version :)
I don't see a list of features to be calculated somewhere, can we add that information into documentation?

issue_title: Need a way to tell StateDownloader.run() to exit
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

That method returns only once its scheduler has run out of requests to make, but we need a way to tell it to stop anyway (e.g. when we get a SIGINT)

### How can it be fixed

Add something like `LightChain._should_stop`, to `StateDownloader`, and have `run()` exit when it is set. 

If we fix #280 first, this should be really straightforward

issue_title: Trinity fails to switch to "regular" sync upon finishing state sync, then hangs indefinitely
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Upon completing state sync (on Ropsten), Trinity attempts to switch to a "regular" rolling sync, but the expected API does not match the one present.

In the log, notice `^C` and the hour-long window in between the timestamps.

Additionally, on subsequent run, starting regular sync fails for the same reason.

``` python
    INFO  06-12 19:22:40       state  Scheduler queue is empty, sleeping a bit
    INFO  06-12 19:22:41       state  Finished state sync with root hash 0x469a557209df85602c0488f90865fa15fa486ec3338fd349f80c685955835421
    INFO  06-12 19:22:41        sync  Starting regular sync; current head: #3423335
   ERROR  06-12 19:22:41     service  Unexpected error in <p2p.sync.FullNodeSyncer object at 0x7fd4cdb38a20>, exiting
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/veox/src/py-evm/p2p/service.py", line 68, in run
    await self._run()
  File "/home/veox/src/py-evm/p2p/sync.py", line 66, in _run
    new_chain = type(self.chain)(self.base_db)
TypeError: __init__() missing 1 required positional argument: 'serializer'
    INFO  06-12 19:22:41      server  Closing server...
    INFO  06-12 19:22:41   discovery  stopping discovery
```

### Wholly optional animal pic

![](https://peepeth.s3-us-west-1.amazonaws.com/images/peep_pics/nJjn8T4s/large.png?1528823327)

Source: [kumaran@peepeth](https://peepeth.com/Kumaran/peeps/QmXiw8HwbufWSG8iSABpeFEY6Dw5PbTVPhE3hrnWiooXxe)

### What is wrong?

Checking transaction signatures in account abstraction involves calculating the hash of the transaction, excluding the `data` field containing the signature itself. This is not possible right now, as there are no opcodes for all transaction fields.

### How can it be fixed

Add an opcode `SIGHASH` with byte value `0x3d` that gets `sha3(rlp(tx \ {data}))`, where `tx \ {data}` refers to the transaction without the data field.

### What is wrong?

Here are the slowest 50 byzantium tests.

The slowest 20 of those combine for 30 minutes worth of test running.  Currently the whole suite takes about 45 minutes to run which is right at the threshhold for travis-ci's maximum runtime for a single test run.

issue_title: source activate without arguments breaks source activate/deactivate commands
issue_body:
Running `source activate` without any further arguments on Linux with the latest release makes it so that further activate/deactivate commands do not work. For example:
```
ihenriksen@ubuntu:~$ source activate
prepending /home/ihenriksen/miniconda2/envs/bin to PATH
ihenriksen@ubuntu:~$ source activate py27
bash: activate: No such file or directory
ihenriksen@ubuntu:~$ source deactivate
bash: deactivate: No such file or directory
ihenriksen@ubuntu:~$
```
This isn't at all a blocking issue for me, but it seems like something worth revisiting when we can.

issue_title: Error parsing VCF format line 'AD' when 'Number=R'
issue_body:
Hi, thanks for pysam! 

There's an issue when parsing the vcf format line:
`##FORMAT=<ID=AD,Number=R,Type=Integer,Description="Number of observation for each allele">`

VCF version 4.2 uses the symbol `R` for representing the fields that have one value for each possible allele (including the reference).
This should include the field `AD` ("Number of observation for each allele") but pysam (versions `0.13` and `0.11.2.2` on OSX) throws an error:
```
ValueError: Formatting error in the format string in line 70: '##FORMAT=<ID=AD,Number=R,Type=Integer,Description="Number of observation for each allele">'
Error BADLY_FORMATTED_FORMAT_STRING: Formatting error in the format string
```
If the `R` is replaced with `.` the vcf is parsed without issue.

The vcf was output from freebayes version `1.1.0`.

Also see this related [issue from gatk](https://gatkforums.broadinstitute.org/gatk/discussion/6919/change-format-of-ad-field-to-number-r).

issue_title: Add support for querying VMSS public IP addresses per VM
Proposal: Replace VCR Playback with Mocked Response in CI
issue_body:
We can very likely preserve the benefits of VCR tests for catching regressions in command table settings and SDK integration without the need to keep and maintain recordings. The current test framework has the following modes and capabilities:
ModeVerify OutputTestsWhere Run
RecordYCLI, SDK, ServiceDev Machine
PlaybackYCLI, SDKCI
LiveYCLI, SDK, ServiceLive Server
What I recommend is something like this:
ModeVerify OutputTestsWhere Run
RecordNCLI, SDK, ServiceDev Machine
PlaybackYCLI, SDKDev Machine
LiveYCLI, SDK, ServiceLive Server
MockNCLI, SDKCI
Functionally, we would be switching the default CI test mode from playing back recordings to mocking the service requests to always assume the sunny-day scenario. It also changes the behavior of Record mode so that the verifications (JMESPathChecks, etc.) are ignored during recording (more on that later). **Considerations** The main benefits: 1. We can use our existing test scenarios with minimal restructuring. 2. We verify the same things as the VCR playback--the CLI behavior and the ability of the SDK to serialize the parameters into the request. 3. We don't need to worry about handling partially randomized names, auth tokens, etc. 4. It will be much faster. 5. No recordings at all--meaning no need to re-record against multiple clouds, etc. The drawbacks: 1. We will have to restructure some tests slightly to work with this. You can't rely on the content of the response to feed into the next command (for example retrieving IDs to use in the --ids field) 2. Commands that make extra REST calls as part of building up the namespace (VM create) will add a wrinkle, but the benefits should outweigh this. Note that while you technically sacrifice "verification"... you really don't because the cassette output is deterministic and will never change. If it worked when it was recorded it will always work when played back, which causes a false sense of security. The Live tests catch this important facet. **Why Retain Playback?** In the proposed mode, Record and Playback mode still exist, but only to support the developer creating Live tests. The flow would be: 1. Run your test in Record mode. This will generate a cassette. Any service failures will fail your test and discard your cassette but errors due to formatting/typos in your verification checks will not cause the recording to fail (because it will ignore those). 2. Run your test in Playback mode. This will test your verifications against the cassettes so you can iteratively test your checks until you are satisfied. At this point the cassette can be discarded. 3. Run your test Live with your verifications in place.

issue_title: Looking for a better way to do things on behalf of another subscription
issue_body:
If I have multiple Azure accounts and multiple subscriptions, and I'm trying to manage all of these with the same backend application using this SDK, I need to go into each subscription and perform the following steps to retrieve the highlighted information:
- Go to the Azure Portal and create a new App Registration.
- When that is complete look at the details of the application and get the **application id**.
- Go to the _Keys_ section, generate a new **secret key** and store that number.
- Go to the _Endpoints_ section - this is very not intuitive btw - and examine the URLs that come up to retrieve the part of the URL that acts as my **tenant id**.
- Look up my subscription details and pull the **subscription id**.

Once all of this is complete, execute an Azure CLI command or PowerShell script that will then grant permissions needed for the new application to do its job.

These are A LOT of numbers to store and keep track of for each subscription. I'd like to see something closer to what Amazon AWS does with IAM Roles, where each account that wants to grant you permissions creates an IAM _Role_ and gives the account that's doing the work a global identifier for that role. That process that does the work then authenticates to Amazon with its own set of secret keys, and performs an _assumeRole_ action to take on the credentials granted by the identifier for that role.

Beyond just permissions, the identifier itself represents a link between two accounts, and the assumeRole action also takes an "external id" field which can be used to further validate the account that's trying to assume those permissions.

This would greatly reduce the complexity of monitoring and management software that we may write for our organizations.

issue_title: api_version is ignored for sub-versioned operations with SdkProfile
issue_body:
The current implementation of profile-versioned operations properties on the main client class will override the requested REST API version in favor of the one defined in the internal default profile.

eg:
```
c = ComputeManagementClient(adalcred, subscription_id=subid, api_version='2016-04-30-preview', base_url=active_cloud.endpoints.resource_manager)

print(c.virtual_machines.api_version)
print(c.disks.api_version)
```

I'd expect to get `2016-04-30-preview` for both of these, but since `disks` is defined in the internal profile, it uses that value first (returning `2017-03-30` in this case). Short of supplying a different profile, there doesn't appear to be a way to get the requested API version for versioned operations sets defined by the default profile.

This can be worked around by passing an empty dictionary for the profile, but it's not intuitive without looking at the SDK source to see the default profile behavior, and I'm not sure if it's "kosher" (eg, is it safe to assume that the profile will always be a simple dictionary, and that empty is OK?)...

issue_title: Some servers don't understand quoted boundaries in multipart documents
issue_body:
## Long story short

After a prolonged debugging session I discovered that the usage of quotes in the boundary of a multipart document caused my request to be rejected by the server since it didn't support this.
Preventing the quotation marks from being added in the code makes this work.

The server in question is using nginx, but I don't know which version yet (asked their support).

## Specification

https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-3.1.1.5
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#appendix-D
https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7230#section-3.2.6

    Content-Type = media-type

    media-type = type "/" subtype *( OWS ";" OWS parameter )
    parameter = token "=" ( token / quoted-string )

    token          = 1*tchar
    quoted-string  = DQUOTE *( qdtext / quoted-pair ) DQUOTE
    qdtext         = HTAB / SP /%x21 / %x23-5B / %x5D-7E / obs-text
    obs-text       = %x80-FF
    quoted-pair    = "\" ( HTAB / SP / VCHAR / obs-text )
    tchar          = "!" / "#" / "$" / "%" / "&" / "'" / "*"
                       / "+" / "-" / "." / "^" / "_" / "`" / "|" / "~"
                       / DIGIT / ALPHA
                       ; any VCHAR, except delimiters

## Expected behaviour

Specify the multipart boundary without quotes.

## Actual behaviour

Specify the multipart boundary with quotes (when they are not needed).

## Steps to reproduce

```py
import asyncio
import pathlib

import aiohttp

SESSION_URL = "http://www.share-online.biz/upv3_session.php"
USER = ""
PASS = ""
path = pathlib.Path(".gitignore")


async def async_main():

        async with session.post(SESSION_URL, data=data) as resp:

        session_name, session_endpoint = text.split(";")

                text = await resp.text()
```

issue_title: `nbgrader feedback` flag to add new feedback file instead of overwriting
issue_body:
Sometimes, I have a back and forth exchanging an assignment with students as they're resolving various issues. In these cases, it would be really helpful to have a flag for `nbgrader feedback` to generate a new feedback file while keeping the previous one (with the original version of the code and my comments) intact. Currently, the only option is to overwrite with `--force`.

Here's roughly the workflow I have in mind:

```sh
# student submits first attempt at notebook.ipynb in ps1
# I provide comments and generate feedback
$ nbgrader feedback ps1
$ find feedback/student/ps1 -name \*.html
notebook.html
# student resolves some of the issues raised in comments, submits new version
# I provide new comments and generate additional feedback
$ nbgrader feedback --add ps1
$ find feedback/student/ps1 -name \*.html
notebook.html
notebook(1).html
# etc.
```

Ideally, when the comments haven't changed, this should be a no-op, so that students don't get confused by having multiple feedback files with the same content. So this would involve always generating the HTML and comparing with the contents of the previous feedback file on disk; if they're the same, no output should be written.

For example, in the following image, all `=` after `>, <, =` are black, and operators `+=, -=, *=, /=` are not recognized.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/826871/28555564-c0866f54-713b-11e7-80a2-46c0d82b756e.png)

There is something wrong in the CodeMirror mode, here is the `doubleOperators` define in `python.js`:

```
var doubleOperators = parserConf.doubleOperators || /^([!<>]==|<>|<<|>>|\/\/|\*\*)/;
```

There is a bug in it for `!=, <=, >=, ==` operators, it should be:

```
var doubleOperators = parserConf.doubleOperators || /^([!<>=]=|<>|<<|>>|\/\/|\*\*)/;
```

and I thinks operators in `doubleDelimiters` should be appended to `doubleOperators`.

Here is the code I added to `codemirror-ipython.js`:

```
pythonConf.doubleOperators = /^([!<>=]=|<>|<<|>>|\/\/|\*\*|\+=|\-=|\*=|%=|\/=|&=|\|=|\^=)/;
pythonConf.doubleDelimiters = /^(@@)/; // I don't know how to remove all items in doubleDelimiters
```
Here is the result (I am using programming ligatures):

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/826871/28555572-ce830e32-713b-11e7-8bd4-8fe4fb79074a.png)
Does not insert on multi-selection.

I had fllowing situation:

```python
from schematics.models import Model
from schematics.types import IntType

class Mapping(Model):
    value = IntType(deserialized_from='@value')

assert Mapping({'@value': '1'}, strict=False).to_primitive() == {'value': 1}
# => {'value': None} != {'value': 1}
```

Here I misspelled `deserialized_from` argument name and it took quite some time to figure it out, because I did not suspected, that `IntType` can swallow arguments with any name.

This happened, because `BaseType` accepts `**kwargs` without using it, which allows all type classes to accept any arguments.

If not used, `**kwargs` should be removed from `BaseType` constructor letting Python do automatic passed argument check.

After removing `**kwargs` I will get nice Python error instead of silence:

```pytb
TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'deserialized_from'
```

Python 3.5, tensorflow 1.5

With Juju storage coming out of experimental, the first hook run on a unit may no longer be the install hook. If initialization such as package installation is left until the install hook, handlers will fail if they are invoked earlier from a storage hook.

One option is to deprecate ```@hook('install')``` in favor of some other decorator doing the moral equivalent of ```@hook('install', '*-storage-attached')```. It wouldn't matter if this is invoked exactly once or potentially multiple times provided the behaviour is documented. Note that a layer does not know the names of the storage declared by the charm using it, and has no syntax available to declare this hook itself.

Another option is I could make a layer, exposing some state allowing handlers to guard from being run too early (or people simply set their own state of course). I suspect this is more error prone in practice.

issue_title: Accept either hashes or IDs for foreign key fields
issue_body:
Entities may be related to each other via one to one or one to many relationships. For example, a product has a one to one relationship with an organization and a one to many relationship with repositories. In other words, a product may belong to one organization and may have many repositories.

The server typically describes these relationships using hashes of data. For example, if one reads a lifecycle environment by issuing an HTTP GET request to `/katello/api/v2/environments/:id`, the server will return a JSON payload in this form:

```json
{
    "name": "…",
    "description": "…",
    "organization": {"id": …, "name": "…"},
}
```

Similarly, one to many relationships are typically described like so:

```json
{
    "organizations": [
        {"id": …, "name": "…"},
        {"id": …, "name": "…"},
    ]
}
```

This is the typical way that relationships are described. But sometimes the server returns IDs instead. For example, if you issue an HTTP GET request to `/katello/api/v2/products/:id`, the server will return a JSON payload in this form:

```json
{
    "gpg_key_id": …,  # one to one
    "sync_plan_id": …,
    "organization": {"id": …, "name": "…"},
}
```

Notice how the one to one relationships on a product are represented using a mixture of IDs and hashes. There's no apparent rhyme or reason here. And one to many relationships may be represented like so:

```json
{
    "related_entity_ids": […, …, …],
}
```

NailGun currently deals with this weirdness by assuming that all relationships are described by hashes and adding in hacks as necessary whenever anything else is done. This makes NailGun less robust and is the cause of much repetitive code in module `nailgun.entities`. Rather than hacking around the hash/ID discrepancies on an individual basis, NailGun should search for both IDs and hashes when trying to populate foreign key fields on entities. Doing so will let NailGun be more robust and let us remove much redundant code from module `nailgun.entities`.

Betelgeuse help statues that `--status` option can accepts `finished` and `inprogress` as possible values but when setting `inprogress` the generated XML does not have the expected property: `<property name="polarion-set-testrun-finished" value="false" />`.

We have a lot of unused branches hanging out. We should do a clean up. 

Can someone to through and assess, when is the last time a branch was touched? (I am sure there are some obvious candidates for destruction.

issue_title: The parameters of CanvasGrid(): grid_row, grid_col and canvas_height, canvas_width are inverted somewhere downstream
issue_body:
As shown on the screenshots below, the parameters for CanvasGrid() seem to be inverted - they in fact are not, but there is (likely) an inversion problem downstream towards rendering on canvas.

GRID_COLS which represents the number of cols in the width comes first, but it is CANVAS_HEIGHT that comes first for the next pair of arguments… (the same goes for GRID_WIDTH and CANVAS_WIDTH)

It also appears that the number of white lines that separate the cells drawn on canvas is determined by the number of rows GRID_ROWS only, thus leading to messing up the display of a non square grid.

(These white lines should be optional, but that is a cosmetic issue.)

The parameter CELL_SIZE which represents the pixel size of a cell on canvas is not used as I think it should: the cell proportion is back-calculated from the ratio of each canvas dimension / number of cells in this dimension, leading to rectangular cells i/o square ones... I think this is okay, but maybe not, depending on the behavior we want.

![grid row cols vs canvas height width](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6275531/13979177/8ad749d8-f111-11e5-9f3e-f1b7adb743a0.png)

#################################################################################

![grid row cols vs canvas height width2](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/6275531/13979184/92f14d9e-f111-11e5-83d0-0b9ae304e286.png)

I took a quick look at the CanvasGrid module and the signature of the initializer looks okay... the error is further down but I did not spot anything immediately obvious.

In reference to the conversation on the mailing list, renaming the variables _row & _col everywhere a reference to the number of cells on the height and the number of cells in the width is made, will improve the code clarity.

Ideas, suggestions, advice?

issue_title: Request for command line argument to set data_file path
issue_body:
Currently, the only option for setting the data_file path is to use either the COVERAGE_FILE environment variable or set the data_file field in .coveragerc. However, neither of these work well for my use case.

In my setup, I'm using Visual Studio Code and have a plugin which runs commands I specify when certain events are triggered. I've configured the following command to run my unit tests and run coverage using pytest-cov: 

`"python -m pytest --cov=Scripts --cov-config ${workspaceRoot}/.coveragerc --cov-report html:${workspaceRoot}/locale/coverage_html --cov-report xml:${workspaceRoot}/local/coverage.xml --cov-report annotate:${workspaceRoot}/local/coverage_annotate ${workspaceRoot}/UnitTests/"`

The issue that because it's VS Codes plugin system performing the actual execution of the command, the working directory at time of execution is C:\Program Files\Microsoft VS Code\, not the directorying containing my Scripts module. This results in Coverage throwing an exception since that area of the file system is write-protected. 

If there were a command line argument to set the data_file path, I could set that in my command here and be all set, and not have to use an environment variable, which I'd like to avoid as it makes the solution harder to migrate to other systems (e.g. our CI system).

issue_title: Ability to build standalone single file executables
issue_body:
# Issue Title
Recently I've been looking into building a small application to solve a problem and I immediately reached for .NET Core. I needed to be able to run the app on Win, Linux and Mac. 

# General
A wrote a small application with a few dependencies. Primarily the EF.Core.SQLite package as I needed to read some information from a local SQLite file. Nothing beyond that. I used the internally available HTTP Client to do some HTTP POST/GET stuff.

Unfortunately I hadn't done my research as I noticed that by default .NET Core Console apps build to an executable dll. Not quite what I had in mind - From a developer UX perspective is this really correct? Surely the common usecase for Console apps would still be an executable that a user would still want to treat as an exe and not as something run from a console with `dotnet MyApp.dll`.

Anywho I soon realised I can use `dotnet publish` to compile an executable and furthermore do so as a standalone by copying the required dlls into the same directory so it can run without any .NET installation on the machine. However I was fairly surprised that there were a total of 240 or so separate DLL files. For my purposes I'm not sure I could distribute my application like this - My users would bulk as having to integrate such a large sum of files. 

For my purposes I really need a single executable file, from a user UX perspective. The file itself can be quite large - The DLLs above weighed in at 60MB, which, is pretty damn large for such a small app, but again not necessarily a pain for me.

I suppose my key point here is: Is there a concerted effort to be able to use .NET Core to create standalone single file (Or at least <5 file) applications?

issue_title: Manual certonly request fails due to newlines in .well-known string
issue_body:
tl;dr: Self-verify of challenge from HTTP server fails; algorithm needs to chomp/trim newlines present in HTTP response.

I am participating in the closed beta and I requested my first cert yesterday.  I am terminating SSL on an external provider so I used the following invocation for letsencrypt-auto:

./letsencrypt-auto -a manual certonly

This process requested that I place a random token at a URL path under /.well-known/acme-challenge/ .  I did so, and tested it with curl, but it still failed to validate.  The error I got was "Self-verify of challenge failed, authorization abandoned."  I ran it again with verbose logging, and noticed this in the log:

2015-11-09 19:11:02,800:DEBUG:acme.challenges:Key authorization from response (u'X6K-MQsdzpMUVNNGoyboRVIxmvfTnNEPbwIoo6Cy2ZU.1WvRR9HsnS_PQRS4VJgCE_gMf8Y28ELCVGXCPlbqVNA
') doesn't match HTTP response (u'X6K-MQsdzpMUVNNGoyboRVIxmvfTnNEPbwIoo6Cy2ZU.1WvRR9HsnS_PQRS4VJgCE_gMf8Y28ELCVGXCPlbqVNA\n\n')

Note the strings are only different because of the newlines.  I generated the file the first time using vi, which added a newline for me.  I had to use "echo -n" to generate the file instead, which wrote the string with no newline.  Many people will embed a newline when writing this file and I think the Let's Encrypt client should be modified to tolerate it.
The `renewer` is over-engineered in places and things such as what can be configured are not well defined.

If `Defaults secure_path` is used in your sudoers file, then `sudo letsencrypt` (e.g. when used in dev env) will not work.

Thanks to @ArchimedesPi to reporting, and @bmw for debugging.

I'm consuming 465k messages to topic A and producing into B within 60 seconds, but I've let it set on flush() for 1 hour with no completion.  Messages appear to be in produced into topic B.  I tried following your recommendations to snehamj with no luck.  During debug it appears to continue going into delivery_callback(err, msg) without end.

Thanks for the help.
 
**Output**
> Copying topic from t-A into t-B
> Topic tsi-A 100,000 messages consumed.
> Topic tsi-A 200,000 messages consumed.
> Topic tsi-A 300,000 messages consumed.
> Topic tsi-A 400,000 messages consumed.
> Topic tsi-A 464,865 messages consumed.
> No more messages to process
> Messages copied: 464,865
> 

**Code**
```
def delivery_callback(err, msg):
    if err:
        sys.stderr.write('%% Message failed delivery: %s\n' % err)
    # else:
    #     sys.stderr.write('%% Message delivered to %s [%d]\n' % (msg.topic(), msg.partition()))


def cpTopic():
    conf = {'group.id': uuid.uuid1(),
            'bootstrap.servers': 'host:9093',
            'security.protocol': 'ssl',
            'ssl.ca.location': 'CARoot.pem',
            'ssl.certificate.location': 'certificate.pem',
            'ssl.key.location': 'key.pem',
            'session.timeout.ms': 6000,
            'compression.codec': 'snappy',
            'default.topic.config': {
                'auto.offset.reset': 'earliest'
            }
            }

    c = Consumer(**conf)
    p = Producer(**conf)
    # Subscribing to a topic will start a background thread
    c.subscribe([topic])

    last = int(time.time())
    timeout = 10
    msg_cnt = 0

    try:
        while True:
            # print 'timeout: ' + str(int(time.time()) - last)
            if (int(time.time()) - last) > timeout:
                print 'Topic %s %s messages consumed.' % (topic, format(msg_cnt, ",d"))
                print 'No more messages to process'
                break

            msg = c.poll(timeout=1.0)
            if msg is None:
                continue
            if msg.error():
                # Error or event
                if msg.error().code() == KafkaError._PARTITION_EOF:
                    # End of partition event
                    continue
                    # sys.stderr.write('Topic %s partition %d reached end at offset %d\n' %
                    #                  (msg.topic(), msg.partition(), msg.offset()))
                elif msg.error():
                    # Error
                    raise KafkaException(msg.error())
            else:
                # Proper message
                msg_cnt += 1
                last = int(time.time())

                if not msg_cnt % 100000:
                    print 'Topic %s %s messages consumed.' % (msg.topic(), format(msg_cnt, ",d"))

                try:
                    p.produce(target_topic, msg.value(), callback=delivery_callback)

                except BufferError as e:
                    sys.stderr.write('%% Local producer queue is full ' \
                                     '(%d messages awaiting delivery): try again\n' %
                                     len(p))
                p.poll(0)


    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print('Aborted by user')

    print 'Messages copied: %s' % format(msg_cnt, ",d")

    # Close down consumer to commit final offsets.
    c.close()
    # Flush messages produced
    p.flush()


if __name__ == "__main__":
    start = time.time()
    topic = sys.argv[1] 
    target_topic = sys.argv[2]  

    print 'Copying topic from %s into %s' % (topic, target_topic)
    cpTopic()
    end = time.time() - start
    print 'Time to complete: {0:.{1}f} seconds'.format(end, 2)
```

issue_title: Remove allow_blank parameter from String fields?
issue_body:
The `allow_blank` was added to address #76 . It was used to determine whether to allow the empty string as a valid value.

This parameter is technically redundant, since the same validation can be achieved using `validate.Length`.

```python
from marshamallow import fields, validate
not_blank = validate.Length(min=1, error='Field cannot be blank')
f = fields.Str(validate=not_blank)
f.deserialize('')  # => Error: Field may not be blank.
```

I propose to remove the `allow_blank` parameter, as it is unnecessary API.

See: https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/marshmallow/schema.py#L800

Decorator `validates_schema(pass_many=True)` does not actually pass the whole collection to the decorated method but still passes them one at time. The reason is at the linked line above (whenever `many==True` objects are passed one by one). Replacing that line with `if many and not pass_many` appears to fix the problem. Also notice the test for this functionality, [here](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/blob/dev/tests/test_decorators.py#L313), still passes because the dictionary passed has 2 items so `len(data) == 2` (even though it is only one data entry, not a collection).

Example, instead of a have this result:

`{"customer": null, "id: "1"}` 

Remove null and result just:

`{"id": "1"}`

issue_title: Serialization drops first item of custom iterators
Allow load to add missing fields with default values
issue_body:
When I have something like this:

```
from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class UserSchema(Schema):
    name = fields.Str(required=True)
    email = fields.Email(required=True)
    created_at = fields.DateTime()
    some_other = fields.Str(default='This is something else')
    
user_data = {
    'created_at': '2014-08-11T05:26:03.869245',
    'email': u'ken@yahoo.com',
    'name': u'Ken'
}
schema = UserSchema()
result = schema.load(user_data)
pprint(result)
```

I currently get:

```
{'created_at': datetime.datetime(2014, 8, 11, 5, 26, 3, 869245),
 'email': 'ken@yahoo.com',
 'name': 'Ken'}
```

I would expect that `some_other` would be added with the default set to `This is something else`, especially if we are passing this to `@post_load` to create an object so that I don't have to have said object also handle setting the default (and potentially getting out of sync).

This is especially handy when using marshmallow to validate and transform the data as necessary on a load before passing it further down the stack to avoid having to add a bunch of `if key in obj` statements.

Currently I end up doing:

```
result = schema.dump(schema.load(user_data))
pprint(result)
```

Which does have the desired result:

```
{'created_at': '2014-08-11T05:26:03.869245+00:00',
 'email': 'ken@yahoo.com',
 'name': 'Ken',
 'some_other': 'This is something else'}
```

but is doing a lot of extra work that shouldn't be necessary.

----

Is there already a recipe for doing something like this with a `@post_load` decorator? If so I don't mind using that.

issue_title: [improvement] Add feature to list all serial ports available on the system (multi-platform)
issue_body:
I saw a good answer @tfeldmann for a generic issue of pyserial [here](http://stackoverflow.com/a/14224477/3753497)

```python
import sys
import glob

import serial


def serial_ports():
    """ Lists serial port names

        :raises EnvironmentError:
            On unsupported or unknown platforms
        :returns:
            A list of the serial ports available on the system
    """
    if sys.platform.startswith('win'):
        ports = ['COM%s' % (i + 1) for i in range(256)]
    elif sys.platform.startswith('linux') or sys.platform.startswith('cygwin'):
        # this excludes your current terminal "/dev/tty"
        ports = glob.glob('/dev/tty[A-Za-z]*')
    elif sys.platform.startswith('darwin'):
        ports = glob.glob('/dev/tty.*')
    else:
        raise EnvironmentError('Unsupported platform')

    result = []
    for port in ports:
        try:
            s = serial.Serial(port)
            s.close()
            result.append(port)
        except (OSError, serial.SerialException):
            pass
    return result


if __name__ == '__main__':
    print(serial_ports())
```

My question for pyserial board is:
Will accept a PR with these feature?
Or Do you prefer a separated pypi package for just this feature?

object FloatType extends TensorDataType

issue_title: Merge environment settings for SSL requests
issue_body:
Hello,
Using v2.3.3, I can't make requests to an SSL endpoint:

```python
import coreapi

client = coreapi.Client()
schema = client.get('https://my-service.url/docs/:443')
```

```
requests.exceptions.SSLError: HTTPSConnectionPool(host='my-service.url', port=443): Max retries exceeded with url: /docs/ (Caused by SSLError(SSLError(1, '[SSL: CERTIFICATE_VERIFY_FAILED] certificate verify failed (_ssl.c:645)'),))
```

According to [this issue](https://github.com/requests/requests/issues/4256) in the requests repo, environment settings need to be explicitely added to the `session.send` call in `coreapi/transports/http.py`:

```python
    def transition(self, link, decoders, params=None, link_ancestors=None, force_codec=False):
        # ...
        settings = session.merge_environment_settings(request.url, None, None, None, None)
        response = session.send(request, **settings)
        result = _decode_result(response, decoders, force_codec)
        # ...
```

Would you take a patch for this? It breaks a few tests, I'll fix them and open a PR if you're ok with it.
Now that tests involving sympy are skipped when sympy is not found, we can have one more test that tests symengine without sympy.

For the following input:

```
(get-info :reason-unknown)
```

z3 prints:

```
(:reason-unknown "(error "line 1 column 11: state of the most recent check-sat command is not known")
```

This isn't SMTLib compliant, since the new standard speculates quote characters should be escaped by using two of them. So, it should look something like:

```
(:reason-unknown "(error ""line 1 column 11: state of the most recent check-sat command is not known")
```

Even then, it looks rather bizarre, perhaps this should simply print as:

```
(:reason-unknown "line 1 column 11: state of the most recent check-sat command is not known")
```

which I guess was the original intent but the `error` part seems to be causing trouble.

issue_title: Compiling a to a statically linked binary
issue_body:
With a tiny change to the build system it's possible to compile to a statically linked binary.  The issue is that `-lrt` needs to appear before `-lpthread` in the linker options on linux. Here's a fix that allows that:

```
diff --git a/scripts/mk_util.py b/scripts/mk_util.py
index 4641a7b..3ece506 100644
--- a/scripts/mk_util.py
+++ b/scripts/mk_util.py
@@ -1613,7 +1613,7 @@ def mk_config():
         config.write('LINK=%s\n' % CXX)
         config.write('LINK_FLAGS=\n')
         config.write('LINK_OUT_FLAG=-o \n')
-        config.write('LINK_EXTRA_FLAGS=-lpthread %s\n' % LDFLAGS)
+        config.write('LINK_EXTRA_FLAGS=%s -lpthread\n' % LDFLAGS)
         config.write('SO_EXT=%s\n' % SO_EXT)
         config.write('SLINK=%s\n' % CXX)
         config.write('SLINK_FLAGS=%s\n' % SLIBFLAGS)
```

With the change applied, the following steps allow building the statically linked binary:

- `LDFLAGS="-static-libstdc++ -static-libgcc -static" CXXFLAGS="-static-libstdc++ -static-libgcc -static" python2 scripts/mk_make.py`
- `cd build; make -j4` as usual

The motivation for linking statically is to make it easy to pass around the binary and to be able to use it in small docker images, such as [wunderseltsam/constraint-solvers](https://registry.hub.docker.com/u/wunderseltsam/constraint-solvers/).

I'd be happy to submit a pull-request against `master` or `unstable` if the change is useful.

issue_title: Integer check in extract does not allow long type
issue_body:
In Python 2.x, the following check does not allow `low` or `high` to be of type `long`:

```_z3_assert(isinstance(high, int) and high >= 0 and isinstance(low, int) and low >= 0, "First and second arguments must be non negative integers")```

You can fix this with `isinstance(high, (int, long))`, for example.

issue_title: pp.decimal for cos, sin, acos, asin, and pi
issue_body:
I'm wondering if/how we could add pretty-printing with pp.decimal for cos, sin, acos, asin, and pi.  Z3 will leave expressions such as `(cos 0.1)` unevaluated when returning a model from get-model or get-value, because they cannot be evaluated exactly.  However, if we have `(set-option :pp.decimal true)`, then we should be able to evaluate these expressions.

Currently this input:
```
(set-option :pp.decimal true)
(declare-const my_cos Real)
(declare-const my_sin Real)
(declare-const my_acos Real)
(declare-const my_asin Real)
(declare-const my_pi Real)
(assert (= my_cos (cos 0.1)))
(assert (= my_sin (sin 0.1)))
(assert (= 0.1 (cos my_acos)))
(assert (= 0.1 (sin my_asin)))
(assert (= my_pi pi))
(check-sat-using qfnra-nlsat)
(get-value (my_cos my_sin my_acos my_asin my_pi))
(get-model)
```
produces this output:
```
sat
((my_cos (cos 0.1))
 (my_sin (sin 0.1))
 (my_acos (+ (acos (- 0.1)) pi))
 (my_asin (acos (- 0.9949874371?)))
 (my_pi pi))
```
Ideally, the output would be something like:
```
sat
((my_cos 0.9950041652?)
 (my_sin 0.0998334166?)
 (my_acos 1.4706289056?)
 (my_asin 0.1001674211?)
 (my_pi 3.1415926535?))
```

This is what I did to get an acceptable solution of managing debug from the CLI:

```python
import os
import pathlib
import click
from flask.cli import FlaskGroup, pass_script_info

import app

@click.group(cls=FlaskGroup, create_app=app.cli_create)
@click.option('--debug/--no-debug', default=False)
@pass_script_info
def main(info, debug):
    # This should probably look at FLASK_DEBUG before overriding it when the 
    # debug flag is not passed to the CLI
    os.environ['FLASK_DEBUG'] = '1' if debug else '0'
```

You can find the issues with setting up debugging by reading this code: https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/master/flask/cli.py#L550-L556 and ultimate https://github.com/pallets/flask/blob/master/flask/helpers.py#L59-L63.

Basically, the only way to kick the debugger on is to set the `FLASK_DEBUG`  environment variable. This main will run prior to the `run` command, so it would be possible to write a simple function to parse/load the configuration here and then set `FLASK_DEBUG` based on that. This seemed like much less hassle and "worked" well enough for me.

Invocation would be `app run --debug` to turn on the debugger.

- **If you have a feature request or idea**, please include a clear description of the use case(s) it would enable, referencing research papers if applicable, and indicate whether you would be willing to implement the feature yourself.

issue_title: Possible to avoid theano.sandbox import during lasagne / layers import?
issue_body:
Hi,
I am using Lasagne in a multi-GPU machine, in which the master process initializes with `theano.config.device=cpu`, then sub-processes are started, and after that each one calls e.g. `theano.sandbox.cuda.use('gpu0')` (or whatever number gpu it is assigned) and then builds its own Theano variables and functions.  The problem I'm having is that this fails if Lasange has been imported in the master process before forking.  I think the reason is the few places within `layers` where `theano.sandbox.cuda` is imported during Lasagne initialization.  

I've gotten around the problem in my case by moving all Lasagne imports inside the initialization of the objects that use them, and I'm careful not to instantiate these until after forking.  I would prefer to be able to leave all the Lasagne imports in the file headers, and have everything work on the GPU as long as I'm careful not to build any Theano variables until after calling `theano.sandbox.cuda.use()`, which is how it can be done with imports of `theano` and `theano.tensor`.

I think this could be achieved by applying the workaround I just described to the few Lasagne layers with the culprit import statements in them.  Does this sound reasonable?  Of course I could do this to my local version (haven't actually tried it yet) but that's no good for sharing code.

Thanks,
Adam

p.s. I've also found it to break when importing `theano.sandbox.rng_mrg`, so it might just be about getting anything from the sandbox.  I'm on bleeding edge Theano and Lasagne and Ubuntu 16.04.

issue_title: Better prompt option, like $_ in cmd.exe
issue_body:
In Windows cmd.exe, by default the prompt is pushed far from the left edge, by display of the directory path. This is awkward and ugly to the eye, as commands of modest length must wrap to the next line.
Not enough people know about the $_ option a cmd.exe window (or in System Environment variables), as in:

> set Prompt=[$P\]$_$+$G$G$S

The above 'set' command changes the prompt from a single line to a double line, due to $_.  Compare the next two prompt examples (1 = single line, 2 = double line):

1.
C:\users\genemi\MyFunDirectory>echo Hello World


2.
[C:\users\genemi\MyFunDirectory\]
>> echo Hello World


So, please enable prompt configuration so commands can start near the left edge, while still displaying the database name or whatever in the preceding line.  :-) 

Thanks. GeneMi @ microsoft. com  (Tuesday  2017/12/12  18:44pm  Redmond time)

issue_title: Any asset on disk can be loaded from output areas
issue_body: This has slices of both "awesome" and "uh oh" as is typical with our environment. :wink: Open up a new notebook from nteract (relative to the repo), with a Python kernel, and run ``` import IPython IPython.display.HTML("") ``` This opens us up to opening any path `../../../../../../../tmp/whatever`. 😧 Realistically, this is how we're loading assets anyways. The problem is that we don't necessarily know if the output of a notebook can/should open a file. After all, on a normal desktop environment, this is _exactly_ what we want for users. They should be able to load images that are relative to their work. The Jupyter notebook server does exactly this (though without implications of directory traversal). The problem it poses is if asset loading can be used in a malicious way from a context like this in Electron. I'm not sure if, when we set things up in iframes in the "not node mode" for scripts, if it will give the same level of isolation to not fetch assets. Is this acceptable behavior? I tend to think so, which implies that we have to do the notebook trust mechanics. I was really hoping we wouldn't have to.

issue_title: Add notebook example for running notebooks from other notebooks and managing error cases
issue_body: Many people requested examples -- perhaps we can upgrade binder to show this pattern well.

I wrote a simple message queue server using ZeroRPC:

```
import zerorpc
import argparse
from daemon import runner
from collections import deque

class Queue(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self._queue = deque()
    def get(self):
        return self._queue.popleft()
    def put(self, value):
        self._queue.append(value)
    def len(self):
        return len(self._queue)

class Application(object):
    def __init__(self):
        self.stdin_path = '/dev/null'
        self.stdout_path = self.stderr_path = args.logfile
        self.pidfile_path = args.pidfile
        self.pidfile_timeout = 5

    def run(self):
        server = zerorpc.Server(Queue())
        server.bind('tcp://{0}:{1}'.format(args.host, args.port))
        server.run()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument('action')
    parser.add_argument('--pidfile', default='/tmp/queued.pid')
    parser.add_argument('--logfile', default='/tmp/queued.log')
    parser.add_argument('--host', default='127.0.0.1')
    parser.add_argument('--port', default='8080')
    args = parser.parse_args()

    app = Application()
    app_runner = runner.DaemonRunner(app)
    app_runner.do_action()
```

The problem with this design is: what happens if, after `Queue.get` returns, the network dies and the message isn't delivered? I'd like to be able to recover the message in that case. So I imagine doing something like:

```
def get(self):
    value = self._queue.popleft()
    try:
        self.return(value)
    except NetworkError:
        self._queue.appendleft(value)
```

Does that make sense? How do you cope with this in your own APIs?

issue_title: Bug in paged_memory.py when deep-copying pages for write
issue_body:
Hi! While analyzing a binary, I ran into an issue of uninitialized memory access (which resulted in a new symbolic variable, which lead to a state explosion). I tracked down the issue to the management of page copies within the machine state. Even though new objects are created for the machine state and the memory, `SimPagedMemory.branch()` uses `new_pages = dict(self._pages)`, which creates shallow copies of the memory pages. This is fine for reads, but the pages need to be deep-copied for writes to avoid overlaps in machine states. As far as I understood, in `SimPagedMemory._get_page()`, if a write is requested, the class checks whether the page in question has been already deep-copied (the page number is listed in `SimPagedMemory._cowed`). If no, than a deep copy is created of the page, and the page number is added to `SimPagedMemory._cowed` to avoid further deep copies.

The VEX IR for the block which triggers the bug: [vex_ir.txt](https://github.com/angr/angr/files/1371297/vex_ir.txt). This IR has 6 successors (of which only 1 will be followed, the jump to `0x24dc0`), and the relevant instructions for the bug are the following:

```
   11 | if (t42) { PUT(ip) = 0x24db0; Ijk_Boring }
   <...>
   30 | if (t59) { PUT(ip) = 0x24db4; Ijk_Boring }
   <...>
   41 | STle(t68) = t51
   <...>
   58 | if (t90) { PUT(ip) = 0x24dc0; Ijk_Boring }
   <...>
   73 | if (t105) { PUT(ip) = 0x24dc4; Ijk_Boring }
   <...>
   77 | STle(t108) = t93
   <...>
   79 | STle(t110) = t95
   <...>
   81 | STle(t112) = t97
   <...>
   107 | if (t139) { PUT(ip) = 0x24dec; Ijk_Boring }
   NEXT: PUT(pc) = 0x00024dd4; Ijk_Boring
}
```

At lines 11 and 30, two successor states are generated from the original, their memory pages are shallow copies of the original's. At line 41, the first write in the block happens, so the original state makes a deep copy of the page in question and adds the page number to `SimPagedMemory._cowed`. At lines 58 and 73, two additional successor states are generated, with shallow copies to the original's memory pages. The bug is triggered at line 77: this write request references the same memory page that has already been deep copied at line 41. Because the page number has already been added to `SimPagedMemory._cowed`, no deep copy is created, the memory page is written to. Even though two successor states (lines 58 and 73) hold shallow copies of the that same memory page.

The issue should be solved by emptying `SimPagedMemory._cowed` whenever a copy is made of the `SimPagedMemory` instance. That should be in `SimPagedMemory.branch()`, but the current version of the code doesn't have this instruction.

As said in [Backward Slicing](http://docs.angr.io/docs/analyses/backward_slice.html):
In[1]:bs = b.analyses.BackwardSlice(cfg, control_flow_sice=True)
Out[1]:
File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/root/.virtualenvs/angr_env/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/angr/analysis.py", line 99, in make_analysis
    oself.__init__(*args, **kwargs)
TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'control_flow_sice'


Is this a bug?

issue_title: sim_proc for strcpy on tainted source seems not to finish
issue_body:
I have a very simple binary (available [here](https://www.ctrlc.hu/~stef/assignment2)) which calls strcpy with argv[1] as source, and dest is a fixed size buffer on the stack. additionally there is a function you_win in the binary, which gets called if i pass ```'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa\x14\x84\x04\x08'``` or equivalent as argv[1].

The following script returns after 3 seconds if i disable sim_procedures with a result of 100 bytes of zero (which makes little sense in this case) and if i enable sim_procedures the script never finishes and keeps step()-ing: 
```
#!/usr/bin/env python

import os
import sys
import angr
from simuvex import s_options as so
import IPython
import logging

l = logging.getLogger("angr")
l.setLevel("INFO")

def fully_symbolic(state, variable):
    '''
    check if a symbolic variable is completely symbolic
    '''
    for i in range(state.arch.bits):
        if not state.se.symbolic(variable[i]):
            return False

    return True

def main(binary):
    binary_name = os.path.basename(binary)

    p = angr.Project(binary, use_sim_procedures=True, load_options={'auto_load_libs':True})

    # argv1 is symbolic
    argv1 = angr.claripy.BVS("argv1",100*8) #since we do not the length now, we just put 100 bytes

    extras = {so.REVERSE_MEMORY_NAME_MAP, so.TRACK_ACTION_HISTORY, so.UNICORN}
    es = p.factory.entry_state(args=[binary,argv1], add_options=extras) #, remove_options={so.LAZY_SOLVES})
    pg = p.factory.path_group(es, veritesting=False, save_unconstrained=True)

    # find a bug giving us control of PC
    l.info("looking for vulnerability in '%s'", binary_name)

    exploitable_path = None
    while exploitable_path is None:
        r = pg.step()
        if r.errored:
            print r.errored
            sys.exit(1)
        if len(pg.unconstrained) > 0:
            l.info("found some unconstrained paths, checking exploitability")
            for u in pg.unconstrained:
                if fully_symbolic(u.state, u.state.regs.pc):
                    exploitable_path = u
                    break

            # no exploitable path found, drop them
            pg.drop(stash='unconstrained')

    if not exploitable_path:
        l.info("no exploitable path found")
        sys.exit(1)

    l.info("found a path which looks exploitable")
    ep = exploitable_path

    assert ep.state.se.symbolic(ep.state.regs.pc), "PC must be symbolic at this point"

    l.info("attempting to create exploit based on found path")
    target=p.loader.main_bin.get_symbol("you_win").addr

    if not ep.state.satisfiable(extra_constraints=[ep.state.regs.pc == target]):
        l.info("cannot point pc to you_win")
        sys.exit(1)

    l.info("pointing pc towards you_win 0x%08x" % target)
    ep.state.add_constraints(ep.state.regs.pc == target)

    l.info("solution: `./%s %s`" % (binary_name, repr(ep.state.se.any_str(argv1))))
    IPython.embed()

    return 0

if __name__ == '__main__':
    if len(sys.argv) > 1:
        sys.exit(main(sys.argv[1]))
    else:
        print "%s: <binary>" % sys.argv[0]
```

What is wrong with my approach or the simuvex implementation of strcpy?
Simplify, which is overloaded in every backends, only prints its result and returns None. Is it a problem if I try to make it return its result ? (if it is possible in its actual implementation)

issue_title: DiracFiles in inputfiles should not be labelled as inputdata in Dirac
issue_body:
When an LFN is listed in the inputsandbox in the Dirac API, the file pointed to is copied to the PWD on the worker node when the job starts. This is waht happens if a `DiracFile` is used a an inputfile for the `Dirac` backend. However, in the line 

https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/8ca6ee73653e32b272d4d5055187d1892f5558e3/ganga/GangaDirac/Lib/RTHandlers/DiracRTHUtils.py#L148

we set it as `inputdata` in the Dirac API as well. This seems wrong. It will cause the job just to be matched to sites where the LFN is present, but that seems wrong in this case (it is typically not a datafile). I suggest to delete the above line.
subfiles for master job not correctly populated from subjobs on develop

I have a feeling this may be a duplicate bug but I can't find anything open if it is.

Ganga doesn't monitor jobs in a 'submitting' state. We briefly did but now this appears to not be working again. I don't know if this is because we now more strongly enforce job state transitions or not but it would be desirable to monitor large jobs which take a long time to submit.

In certain situations, including when ganga is updated, the `~/.gangarc` is automatically updated with a newer version. The old file is renamed to avoid collision and preserve the contents.

In my situation the `~/.gangarc` file is a symlink pointing to a shared location. This allows me to have the same settings across multiple machines. However when the update cycle kicks in, I have to manually fix the symlink and verify any relevant content changes. More often than not, content didn't actually change.

Would it be possible to migrate the content instead of renaming the file (i.e. `cat ~/.gangarc > ~/.gangarc.old` ; `update ~/.gangarc`) or at least provide some kind of confirmation prompt before action?

issue_title: Possible source of lockups with GangaObject._getWriteAccess
issue_body:
I've just fixed a lockup in Tasks where the Task Monitor thread but I'm concerned this could cause problems in the future. The issue is this:

1. Object (Job or Task) is NOT loaded
2. thread calls `obj._getWriteAccess` which explicitly gets the `const_lock`
3. During this, it also starts to load the object which at various times will try to get the `internal_lock`
4. In another thread, the object is accessed which grabs the `internal_lock`
5. After this, it calls `obj._getReadAccess` attempt to load.
6. This then tries to grab the `const_lock`.

The order is not consistent in the two cases and so can lead to deadlocks.

This was a problem in Tasks because of the specific call to `_getWriteAccess` but I'm concerned this could happen elsewhere. I'm not sure how to proceed with this but I thought I'd put in an issue so we remember :smile:

```
cd doc/Doxygen_API/
doxygen Doxyfile
```

2. Update `Doxyfile` to
```
INPUT                  = include \

                         src \
```

issue_title: Plot individual cell traces by indicating relative index within population
Files ending in .gz are delivered with incorrect content headers
issue_body:
Files ending in .gz served up by pulp.server.content.web.views are served with an incorrect content type and encoding.

This affects .xml.gz files generated by yum-distributor. For example:

```
$ curl -v http://192.168.121.51/pulp/repos/f04a1d5b-32d8-4a60-a580-cc158ab25004/repodata/5f90923d6f2e4b3d0f27c13e927821b9a7001b349f99222752c37496a40531d8-updateinfo.xml.gz > /dev/null
*   Trying 192.168.121.51...
  % Total    % Received % Xferd  Average Speed   Time    Time     Time  Current
                                 Dload  Upload   Total   Spent    Left  Speed
  0     0    0     0    0     0      0      0 --:--:-- --:--:-- --:--:--     0* Connected to 192.168.121.51 (192.168.121.51) port 80 (#0)
> GET /pulp/repos/f04a1d5b-32d8-4a60-a580-cc158ab25004/repodata/5f90923d6f2e4b3d0f27c13e927821b9a7001b349f99222752c37496a40531d8-updateinfo.xml.gz HTTP/1.1
> User-Agent: curl/7.40.0
> Host: 192.168.121.51
> Accept: */*
> 
< HTTP/1.1 200 OK
< Date: Thu, 17 Mar 2016 03:24:50 GMT
< Server: Apache/2.4.18 (Fedora) OpenSSL/1.0.2g-fips mod_wsgi/4.4.8 Python/2.7.10
< Last-Modified: Thu, 17 Mar 2016 00:57:43 GMT
< ETag: "278-52e341e355108" 
< Content-Length: 632
< Content-Type: text/xml
< 
```

The server incorrectly claims that the response content is Content-Type: text/xml, with no Content-Encoding. It should be: Content-Type: text/xml, Content-Encoding: gzip.


For more information check https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1781

issue_title: As a user i can manage modular content
issue_body:
Sync/publish scenario
---------------------------
1. create and sync modular repo
2. validate that in the repo there are identified modulemd and modulemd_defaults.
3. publish repo.
4. validate that compressed yaml doc is available in the repodata
5. test dnf client is able to install module from the metadata produced by pulp

Sync/copy scenario ( modulemd)
------------------------
1. create and sync modular repo
2. copy module A , ensure destination repo has module A
3. copy recursively module B, ensure destination repo has modulee B + its rpms( artifacts)

Sync/remove scenario ( modulemd)
---------------------------
1. create and sync modular repo
2. remove module A, ensure its rpms are removed from the repo as well. Only not cross referenced rpms in other modules within the repo should be removed. This way we ensure we do not leave after us ursine modular rpm. ( ursine == modular rpm not belonging to any module)

Sync/copy scenario ( modulemd_defaults)
------------------------
1. create and sync modular repo
2. copy modulemd-defauls A , ensure destination repo has modulemd-defaults A. Nothing else is carried over.

Sync/copy/upload scenario ( modulemd_defaults)
------------------------
1. create and sync modular repo
2. copy modulemd-defauls A , ensure destination repo has modulemd-defaults A. Nothing else is carried over.
3. upload modulemd-defaults A( same unit key, different content) yaml file. Ensure destination repo has just one modulemd-defaults A (we can have just one defaults of module A per repo).

Sync/remove scenario ( modulemd_defaults)
---------------------------
1. create and sync modular repo
2. remove modulemd-defaults A, ensure it was removed from the repo, Nothing else is removed.

Upload scenario ( modulemd and defaults)

1. upload a modules.yaml doc file that would containt 1+ of modulemd and 1+ of modulemd_defaults.
2. Ensure that pulp was able to parse the document by identifying correct content type `modulemd` and `modulemd_defaults`

Rest API
http://pulp.readthedocs.org/en/latest/dev-guide/integration/rest-api/content/associate.html#unassociating-content-units-from-a-repository

- [x]  category     - remove package categories from a repository
- [ ]   distribution - remove distributions from a repository
- [ ]   drpm         - remove DRPMs from a repository
- [ ]   environment  - remove package environments from a repository
- [x]   errata       - remove errata from a repository
- [x]   group        - remove package groups from a repository
- [ ]   metafile     - remove yum metadata files from a repository
- [x]   rpm          - remove RPMs from a repository
- [ ]   srpm         - remove SRPMs from a repository

Example for removing an errata.

https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1535

Create a repository
 
pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id=foo  --feed=https://repos.fedorapeople.org/repos/pulp/pulp/demo_repos/zoo/

Synchronize the repository
pulp-admin rpm repo sync run --repo-id=foo

List the errata
pulp-admin rpm repo content errata --repo-id foo

Remove an errata
pulp-admin -vv rpm repo remove errata --repo-id=foo --in='id=RHEA-2010:0002'

```
class Heating(graphene.ObjectType):
```

1. Open the folder with the tensorboard files in it
2. Open a command window from this folder (this folder as root)

For me it would be a huge quality of life improvement to make `logdir` default to the current folder (`.`).

 - Allow "region" to be an array for multi-region deploys (probably the harder solution)

I'm leaning towards a `--region` cli option.

issue_title: Cfn-Sphere fails to update Stacks with nested stacks
issue_body: If cfn-sphere has to wait on create_complete or update_complete events for stacks containing nested stacks, it finishes to early. Reason is a bug in the event filtering. We currently only wait for events of type `AWS::CloudFormation::Stack` with status `*_COMPLETE`. When a nested stack resource completes it's creation, an event matching the given criteria occurs for the root stack. The only difference is the logical resource id stating that this event is for the nested stack.

From https://github.com/pyFFTW/pyFFTW/issues/106#issuecomment-322802960

Ingredients:

- OS Linux 4.4.0-92, Ubuntu):

- conda base packages (miniconda):
>     numpy 1.13.1 py36_0
>     python 3.6.2 0
>     mkl 2017.0.3 0

- pyFFTW compiled via pip:
>     pyFFTW (0.10.4)

- pyFFTW installed from conda-forge:
>     pyfftw 0.10.4 np113py36_1 conda-forge


## **The problem**: numpy is faster than conda-forge pyFFTW!

    Numpy (with mkl)
```
In [1]: import numpy as np
In [2]: x=np.random.random(4096)
In [3]: %timeit np.fft.fft(x)
79.1 µs ± 661 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

    Locally compiled pyFFTW from pip, works great:
```
In [6]: import pyfftw
In [7]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [8]: a[:] = x
In [9]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [10]: %timeit fftobj()
15.4 µs ± 443 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

BTW, it speeds up substantially also with interfaces, and I didn't even switch on the cache.

    conda-forge pyFFTW
```
In [1]: import numpy as np
In [2]: import pyfftw
In [3]: x=np.random.random(4096)
In [4]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [5]: a[:] = x
In [6]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [7]: %timeit fftobj()
110 µs ± 202 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
In [8]: %timeit np.fft.fft(x)
77.9 µs ± 73.2 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

   conda-forge pyFFTW, reordering imports:
```
In [1]: import pyfftw
In [2]: import numpy as np
In [3]: x=np.random.random(4096)
In [4]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [5]: a[:] = x
In [6]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [7]: %timeit fftobj()
111 µs ± 427 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

   conda-forge pyFFTW, MKL-unsupported transform:
```
In [1]: import pyfftw
In [2]: import numpy as np
In [3]: shape = (1, 1, 4096)
In [4]: x=np.random.random(np.prod(shape)).reshape(shape)
In [5]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [6]: a[:] = x
In [7]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [9]: %timeit fftobj()
116 µs ± 5.72 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
In [10]: %timeit np.fft.fft(x)
78 µs ± 730 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

   same on a larger array:
```
In [9]: shape = (100, 1, 4096)
In [10]: x=np.random.random(np.prod(shape)).reshape(shape)
In [11]: a = pyfftw.empty_aligned(x.shape, dtype='float64')
In [12]: a[:] = x
In [13]: %timeit np.fft.fft(x)
7.87 ms ± 9.75 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)
In [14]: fftobj = pyfftw.builders.fft(a)
In [15]: %timeit fftobj()
11.3 ms ± 12.2 µs per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 100 loops each)
```

So, at the moment, I am actually using pyFFTW from pip (locally compiled), and use it for 1D transforms (or 2D over 2 axes, etc), whereas I use numpy.fft for other transforms.

issue_title: Starter stuck when job is failed/done too quickly
issue_body:
Consider the example when job has failed too quickly (for instance, due to the invalid parameter value), in such case job status will be changed to `FAILED` and stuck forever (with running processes). It will similarly stuck when job is successfully finished. 

To replicate use the following script and run it with starter `aetros run --local`:
```python
import aetros.backend

if __name__ == '__main__':
    job = aetros.backend.context()
    raise IOError("File not found")
```
However, if you run it with `python quick.py` it will finish properly (i.e. status will be changed to `STOPPED`), which suggests that the problem is with aetros starter.
Interestingly, that when you run it in debug mode `DEBUG=1 aetros run --local` or add delay `sleep(1)` after job initialisation, it will also finish properly. Might be something related to race conditions / async issues (i.e. when job hasn't initialised properly but already finished)

issue_title: No option to turn off pipeline estimators with None in params
issue_body:
#### Description
Unlike the regular [scikit-learn Pipeline object](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/pipeline.html) there is no option to turn off an estimator in the parameter grid (with None). This is extremely useful for model selection using (Gridsearch) crossvalidation.

Either the imblearn Pipeline object is to be changed, or the imblearn transformers as to be compatible with sklearn's Pipeline.

#### Steps/Code to Reproduce
```
from imblearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.models import svm
from imblearn.over_sampling import SMOTE

estimators = [('resample', SMOTE()), ('classify', svm.SVC())]
pipe = Pipeline(estimators)
param_grid = {'resample': [None, SMOTE()],}

gridsearch = GridSearch(pipe, param_grid)

gridsearch.fit(X, y)
```

#### Expected Results
Regular old gridsearch starts with None estimator enabled

#### Actual Results
Error saying None is not an estimator object.

#### Versions
Linux-4.4.0-59-generic-x86_64-with-Ubuntu-16.04-xenial
('Python', '2.7.12 (default, Jul  1 2016, 15:12:24) \n[GCC 5.4.0 20160609]')
('NumPy', '1.12.0')
('SciPy', '0.18.1')
('Scikit-Learn', '0.18.1')
('Imbalanced-Learn', '0.2.1')

issue_title: custom object deleter is not called
Is there any suitable auto generator for large existing project?
issue_body: Hi, currently I'm using Boost.Python + pyplusplus  to deal with large existing C/C++ project by auto generation, Is it possible to migrate pyplusplus to pybind11?

From http://blog.zulip.org/2016/10/13/static-types-in-python-oh-mypy/:

Add the  --check-untyped-defs option to your mypy arguments, and get that running with no errors on the codebase. This option causes mypy to check every def in the codebase for internal consistency; mypy can detect many classes of bugs and mistakes in your codebase, without your having written a single type annotation!

In most cases, you’ll want to fix bugs and bad code as you go, but you can also use  #type: ignore annotations or exclude files to defer issues. For example, we excluded all of Zulip’s tests at first, since they’re both lower value to type-check and had most of the monkey-patching and other questionable Python in the project. For Zulip, getting clean  --check-untyped-defs output took me about 2 days of hard work, including merging fixes for about 40 issues in the Zulip codebase.

I spent another day or two generating nice reproducers for the mypy bugs I’d encountered and improving typeshed. Now that mypy is no longer in its infancy, mypy bugs are increasingly rare. But a large project should expect to encounter and fix typeshed issues (just submit a PR!).

issue_title: no way to define non-standard home directories
issue_body:
Hi, I'm using jupyterhub with systemdspawner on a linux system where the home directories are mounted using some scheme that I have no control over and don't really understand (I have sudo privileges, but don't administer things like users).  The home directories for the users are *not* /home/{USERNAME}, and they are *not* all under the same parent directory.  E.g., my home directory is under /u/af/ci/{USERNAME}, but others are under /u/xx/yy/{USERNAME}, substitute whatever two letter string for xx and yy.

issue_title: stdout and stderr of master task
issue_body:
Right now each task writes to its own `.out` and `.err` file. This works ok for single tasks but can be troublesome for master tasks that execute multiple tasks in parallel.

Currently each subtask will write to their own `.out` and `.err` files so the master `.out` and `.err` files will be empty, and no error will be reported if one or more subtasks fail. Allowing all subtasks to write to the master `.out` and `.err` files is also problematic because of lock/race condition etc.

I think a proper solution would be collecting all `.out` and `.err` files after subtasks have been completed.

issue_title: Handle other iterable sequences as "param[]=..." parameter values
issue_body:
Issue #55 pointed out that `canvasapi` 0.6.0 made it awkward to pass a `list` as a parameter value. Pull request #63 fixed this, with friendly support for both `list` and `tuple` from `canvasapi` 0.7.0 forward. Yay! However, `list` and `tuple` are just two of the many Pythonic ways to collect multiple values. It would be nice if `canvasapi` provided the same parameter-marshaling support for any parameter value that is actually an iterable collection of multiple values: `set`, generators, iterable objects returned by `zip` and `ichain`, etc. Note, however, that strings are a special case: `str` is iterable, but should be treated as a single value, not as a sequence of isolated characters.

An example of something that works in `canvasapi` 0.7.0:

``` python
course.list_multiple_submissions(student_ids='all', assignment_ids=[1, 2, 3])
```

Some examples of things that do *not* work in `canvasapi` 0.7.0:

``` python
# multiple values as a set
assignment_ids = set(...)
course.list_multiple_submissions(student_ids='all', assignment_ids=assignment_ids)

# multiple values as an iterator over dictionary values
mapping = {'a': 1, 'b': 2, 'c': 3}
course.list_multiple_submissions(student_ids='all', assignment_ids=mapping.values())

# multiple values as a generator expression
course.list_multiple_submissions(student_ids='all', assignment_ids=(assn.id for assn in ...))
```

<hr/>

Spoiler alert: I am already developing code to implement this feature. Expect a pull request soonish.

I've noticed that simple stack operations are slower than they used to be. I don't know if this is because of unindexed fields, that we're just scanning more data, or what. But it's very noticeable especially as I've been demoing. For a project with a single stack, I am seeing 6 seconds for `stack ls`:

```
 time pulumi stack ls
NAME                                             LAST UPDATE              RESOURCE COUNT     URL
gcp-gke-dev                                      20 hours ago             3                  https://app.pulumi.com/joeduffy/gcp-gke-dev

real	0m5.871s
user	0m0.154s
sys	0m0.035s
```

Rerunning it over and over again, I get varying response times, but always multi-second and far larger than I would expect. Frankly, I'd have expected this operation to take 50ms or, worse case, 200ms:

```
real	0m2.658s
real	0m5.217s
real	0m3.207s
real	0m5.963s
real	0m6.775s
real	0m4.058s
```

From some crude profiling, it appears there are four service API calls:

*1. /user*

Surprisingly, the call to the user endpoint seems to take the longest of the bunch.

```
I0827 15:38:22.717625   83556 api.go:157] Pulumi API call details (https://api.pulumi.com/api/user): headers=map[Authorization:[token <snip>] Content-Type:[application/json] User-Agent:[pulumi-cli/1 (v0.15.1-dev-1535154609-ga0cf4151; darwin)]]; body=
I0827 15:38:26.719648   83556 api.go:170] Pulumi API call response code (https://api.pulumi.com/api/user): 200 OK
```

*2. /user/stacks/?project=...*

The project call seems to be the second-lengthiest (usually two seconds).

```
I0827 15:38:26.723162   83556 api.go:157] Pulumi API call details (https://api.pulumi.com/api/user/stacks?project=gcp-gke): headers=map[User-Agent:[pulumi-cli/1 (v0.15.1-dev-1535154609-ga0cf4151; darwin)] Authorization:[token <snip>] Content-Type:[application/json]]; body=
I0827 15:38:28.422431   83556 api.go:170] Pulumi API call response code (https://api.pulumi.com/api/user/stacks?project=gcp-gke): 200 OK
```

*3. /user*

I'm not sure why we need to hit the user endpoint twice?

```
I0827 15:38:28.422772   83556 api.go:157] Pulumi API call details (https://api.pulumi.com/api/user): headers=map[Content-Type:[application/json] User-Agent:[pulumi-cli/1 (v0.15.1-dev-1535154609-ga0cf4151; darwin)] Authorization:[token <snip>]]; body=
I0827 15:38:28.621618   83556 api.go:170] Pulumi API call response code (https://api.pulumi.com/api/user): 200 OK
```

*4. /stacks/.../export*

The export seems to be pretty quick, although there's definitely some slowness in processing the full checkpoint information that comes back (a vast subset of which is actually needed for `stack ls`).

```
I0827 15:38:28.621814   83556 api.go:157] Pulumi API call details (https://api.pulumi.com/api/stacks/joeduffy/gcp-gke-dev/export): headers=map[Content-Type:[application/json] User-Agent:[pulumi-cli/1 (v0.15.1-dev-1535154609-ga0cf4151; darwin)] Authorization:[token <snip>]]; body=
I0827 15:38:28.685756   83556 api.go:170] Pulumi API call response code (https://api.pulumi.com/api/stacks/joeduffy/gcp-gke-dev/export): 200 OK
```

If there is low-hanging fruit here (like the unneeded second `/user` call), let's fix it. But that first call to `/user` taking so long is slightly troubling since we hit this endpoint all the time.

issue_body:
From here https://pypi.python.org/simple/ssh2-python/ it looks like the `0.11` version of `ssh2-python` doesn't exists as source (.tar.gz) nor linux wheel. Something went wrong with the build ?

By the way, openssl `1.0.2n` is not available anymore since today, need to upgrade to openssl `1.0.2o`

Currently there are many component docstrings on __init__, __call__ or array_call methods which don't really say much of anything useful that the base class docstrings don't already say. For those components, we should remove the docstring entirely so that Sphinx will use the base class docstrings (which are written to be generic enough to apply to any component). The important part of the documentation is present in the *_properties dictionaries of the components.

We also could think about a way to include those property dictionaries in the documentation, but that's a separate issue.

I was trying to copy an existing HDF5 file to MRC format. The HDF5 file is too big. I could use the existing mechanism to dump the whole HDF5 dataset to an MRC file as:

    with mrcfile.new('tmp.mrc') as mrc:
        mrc.set_data(large_hdf5[...], dtype=np.float32))

However, that requires me to load the whole HDF5 file into memory. A similar problem arises if I want to initialize an empty MRC file (similar to `np.zeros`) of size larger than RAM (e.g. 4000x4000x4000), it would require me to create `np.zeros((4000,4000,4000))` beforehand, which is a bit redundant.

This can be solved using memmaps, which is what I tend to use when h5py is not available/needed.

By inspection I managed to come up with the following code, which will initialize an empty dataset without requiring to create it with numpy beforehand:

    import mrcfile as mrc
    import numpy as np

    def newmrc(fpath, shape, dtype, fill=None):
        f = mrc.MrcMemmap(fpath, mode='w+', overwrite=True)
        fsize = f.header.nbytes + f.header.nsymbt + np.prod(shape) * dtype2size(dtype)
        f._iostream.truncate(fsize)
        f._open_memmap(dtype, shape)
        f.update_header_from_data()
        f.update_header_stats()
        if fill is not None:
            f.data[...] = fill
        return f

That would allow you to create a memmaped MRC without having to hold a very large array in memory beforehand. You could then apply any chunking mechanism to write the full file.

I thought it was useful to share the script, because 1) maybe I'm missing something important or 2) it might be useful to add to the library itself.
